worth_it_explanation: Blocking controls decide which advertisers, categories, and creatives are allowed to clear on a publisher's inventory, so they sit at the point where brand safety and revenue meet. A block list that no longer matches the publisher's standards lets through advertisers it meant to exclude, and a block list that grew too broad suppresses demand the publisher could have kept. The check is a review of the block settings a publisher already holds in Equativ, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete list the publisher can tighten or trim.
intro: Creative blocking decides which advertisers, advertiser categories, and creatives are allowed to win an impression on a publisher's inventory in Equativ (formerly Smart AdServer). This subcategory is about the block lists themselves, what they exclude and whether the exclusions still fit, rather than the verification vendors or contextual signals that feed them. These checks confirm that the block lists match the publisher's current brand standards and that they are tightened to the inventory at risk rather than spread so wide that they suppress demand the publisher could keep.

audit_checks.title: Block lists no longer match the publisher's brand standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block list set once and left alone drifts away from the publisher's standards as advertisers, categories, and creatives change. When the list lags the standards, advertisers the publisher means to exclude can clear on its inventory, which puts the publisher's relationship with its audience and its direct-sold partners at risk over content it never agreed to run.

educate.base:
Equativ (formerly Smart AdServer) gives a publisher several ways to keep an advertiser or a creative off its inventory. A publisher can block by advertiser domain, the adomain value the buy carries, so a named advertiser cannot win regardless of which campaign it runs. It can block by advertiser category, using the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) content categories that classify the buy, so a whole sensitive topic stays out. It can block individual creatives once they have been seen and judged unsuitable. Each control answers a different question, and a publisher's brand standards usually rely on more than one of them at the same time.

The block list is only as useful as it is current. Brand standards are not fixed. A publisher signs a direct deal that requires a competitor to be excluded, drops a category it once allowed, or reviews a creative that slipped through and decides it should not run again. Each of those is a change to the standard, and each one needs a matching change to the block list. When the list is set once and left alone, it falls behind the standard it was meant to enforce, and the gap is invisible until an advertiser the publisher meant to exclude shows up in a report.

The audit question is whether the block lists in Equativ still reflect the standards the publisher holds today. That means checking the advertiser-domain blocks, the category blocks, and the creative blocks against the publisher's current rules, not against the rules that were in place when the list was first built.

A few signs point to block lists that have fallen behind the standard:
  • The block list has no record of when it was last reviewed or who owns keeping it current
  • Direct-sold deals that require a competitor exclusion are not reflected in the advertiser-domain blocks
  • Reporting shows advertisers or categories clearing that the publisher's written standards say should be excluded
You may want to confirm that the block lists match the standards the publisher holds now. One approach is to review each block list against the current brand rules and the active direct deals, then set an owner and a review cadence so the list keeps pace with the standard.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, editorial credibility depends on the audience trusting what runs next to the content, so an advertiser or category that conflicts with the publication's standards does more damage than lost revenue. You may want to review the category and advertiser-domain blocks against the publication's editorial guidelines and any advertiser exclusions its direct deals require, so the block list protects the relationship with readers rather than lagging it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the block list often needs to exclude direct competitors so a rival's ad does not run beside the publisher's own product content. You may want to confirm that competitor domains and competing product categories are in the advertiser blocks, and that the list is updated as the publisher's own catalog and its competitive set change.

learn_more.source_title: OpenRTB standards (adomain and advertiser block-list compliance)
Content Taxonomy 2.2 for brand safety and brand suitability

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser, category, and creative blocking is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the advertiser-domain, category, and creative block lists in Equativ against the publisher's current brand standards and active direct deals, and look for any record of when each list was last reviewed.

equativ_ui.label: Equativ console
steps: Open the brand safety or blocking settings and list the active advertiser-domain blocks, category blocks, and creative blocks.
Compare each block list against the publisher's written brand standards and the advertiser exclusions its direct deals require, and note any standard that has no matching block.
Ask the publisher when each list was last reviewed and who owns keeping it current, and record the answer.
Note any advertiser or category the standards say should be excluded that the block list does not cover.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by advertiser domain and advertiser category for inventory that ran through the open auction.
Compare the advertisers and categories that cleared against the publisher's exclusion standards to see whether anything the standard excludes still won impressions.
Record any advertiser or category that cleared in conflict with the standard, with the inventory and time period where it appeared.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block list, when it was last reviewed, and whether it matches the publisher's current standards and active direct deals. Block lists that reflect the current standards, with an owner and a review cadence on record, pass. A block list that lags the standard, shown by an excluded advertiser or category clearing in reporting or by a deal exclusion missing from the list, is a fail recorded with the advertisers, categories, or deals affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block list, its last review date, and whether it matches the current standards and direct deals above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the block lists on {{site_name}} no longer match the brand standards the publisher holds today, so advertisers or categories the standards exclude can still clear on its inventory. Brand standards change as direct deals are signed, categories are dropped, and creatives are reviewed, and a block list that is set once and left alone falls behind those changes until an excluded advertiser shows up in a report. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the advertiser-domain, category, and creative blocks in Equativ against the current brand rules and active direct deals, then setting an owner and a review cadence so the list keeps pace with the standard. After the review, recheck reporting to confirm no excluded advertiser or category is still clearing.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are over-broad and suppress demand needlessly
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block list that grew wider than the publisher's standards require removes demand that was safe to run, which thins the auction and lowers the clearing price across affected inventory. Broad category blocks and large advertiser-domain lists added over time without review can suppress revenue well beyond the small slice of demand they were meant to exclude.

educate.base:
A block list protects a publisher by removing demand it does not want, and every block removed is demand that can no longer compete for the impression. That is the point when the block matches a real standard. It becomes a cost when the block is wider than the standard requires because the auction loses bidders that were safe to run, and a thinner auction clears at a lower price. Brand safety and yield pull against each other here, so the question is not whether to block but whether each block is sized to the risk.

Block lists drift wide for ordinary reasons. A whole Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) category gets blocked to keep out one objectionable subtopic, when a narrower category or a creative-level block would have done the job. Advertiser domains get added during a one-time dispute and never removed once it is settled. A block copied from another property carries exclusions that do not apply to this inventory. None of these is wrong on its own, but together they can grow a list that excludes far more demand than the publisher's standards actually call for.

The audit question is whether each block earns its place. A block that maps to a written standard, a regulatory rule, or an active direct-deal exclusion is doing its job. A block with no standard behind it, or one set so wide that it removes demand the publisher would accept, is a candidate to narrow or remove. Equativ's reporting can show how much demand a block is removing, which turns the trade between safety and revenue into a number the publisher can weigh.

A few signs point to block lists that are over-broad:
  • A full advertiser category is blocked when only a narrower subtopic or a single creative is the concern
  • The advertiser-domain block list has entries no one can tie to a current standard or deal
  • Inventory under heavy category blocks shows a noticeably thinner auction and lower clearing price than comparable unblocked inventory
You may want to confirm that each block maps to a real standard and is sized to the risk. One approach is to review the block list entry by entry, narrow or remove the blocks that have no standard behind them, and read the change in fill rate and clearing price on the affected inventory.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a broad category block added to keep one sensitive subtopic off the news section can also strip safe demand from lifestyle or entertainment sections where the subtopic never appears. You may want to confirm that category blocks are applied at the level of inventory that actually carries the risk rather than across the whole site, so safe sections keep their demand.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, blocking a competitor's whole product category to keep one rival out can also remove demand from adjacent advertisers the publisher would happily run. You may want to confirm that competitor exclusions are scoped to the specific domains in the competitive set rather than to a category that sweeps in safe demand alongside the rival.

Content Taxonomy 2.2 for brand safety and brand suitability

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser, category, and creative blocking is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each block list entry against a current standard or deal, and read how much demand the broad blocks are removing from the affected inventory.

equativ_ui.label: Equativ console
steps: Open the brand safety or blocking settings and list every advertiser-domain block and category block with its scope.
For each entry, ask the publisher which written standard, regulatory rule, or direct-deal exclusion it maps to, and record any entry that has no standard behind it.
Note any case where a full category is blocked to exclude a narrower subtopic, or where a block applies across all inventory rather than the slice that carries the risk.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by inventory, with fill rate, bid density, and average clearing price.
Compare inventory under heavy category or advertiser blocks against comparable unblocked inventory to see how much the blocks thin the auction and lower the clearing price.
Record the revenue effect of the broad blocks so the trade between the exclusion and the demand it removes can be weighed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block list entry, the standard or deal it maps to, and the demand it removes from the affected inventory. A block list where each entry maps to a current standard and is scoped to the inventory at risk passes. A list with entries that map to no standard, or broad blocks that remove demand the publisher would accept, is a fail recorded with the entries to narrow or remove and the inventory where they cost fill and price.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block list entry, the standard it maps to, and the demand it removes above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the block lists on {{site_name}} are wider than the publisher's standards require, so they remove demand that was safe to run and thin the auction on the affected inventory. Each block excludes a bidder, which protects the publisher when the block matches a real standard but lowers the clearing price when the block is wider than the risk calls for. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the block list entry by entry in Equativ, narrowing or removing the blocks that map to no current standard or deal, and reading the change in fill rate and clearing price on the affected inventory. After the change, confirm that the remaining blocks each map to a standard and that the safe demand has returned to the auction.
